title: FOCUS: Object-Centric Evidence as an Update-Safety Signal for Realistic Online VLM Adaptation
description: FOCUS is a lightweight wrapper around StatA for realistic online CLIP adaptation. It tests a narrow claim: whether object-centric evidence from full-image, foreground, and background views is a better signal for deciding when a sample is safe to update on than entropy, max-softmax, or an existing reliability-style gate at the same acceptance rate.
motivation: Recent realistic online VLM adaptation work shows that pseudo-label mistakes can poison later updates, while context-bias papers show that CLIP often gets the right label for the wrong reason. The gap is not the absence of reliability filtering or foreground-background decomposition in general; it is the lack of a targeted study of whether object-centric evidence is a better update-safety signal for StatA-style online streams.
proposed_approach: Wrap StatA with a cheap object-centric gate built from full/foreground class agreement, foreground support for the predicted class, and background dominance. Evaluate that gate only as an update-acceptance mechanism, compare it against entropy, max-softmax, a ReTA-style consistency gate, and simple object-centric variants under matched acceptance-rate control, and use a sparse short-horizon counterfactual analysis to measure harmful updates within the one-GPU budget.

related_work.title: Realistic Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models
related_work.authors: Maxime Zanella, Clément Fuchs, Christophe De Vleeschouwer, Ismail Ben Ayed
related_work.how_it_relates: Defines the realistic online VLM TTA setting and introduces StatA, which FOCUS wraps rather than replaces.

related_work.title: A Lost Opportunity for Vision-Language Models: A Comparative Study of Online Test-Time Adaptation for Vision-Language Models
related_work.authors: Mario Döbler, Robert A. Marsden, Tobias Raichle, Bin Yang
related_work.how_it_relates: Shows online VLM TTA is brittle and evaluation-sensitive, motivating a narrow claim focused on update safety instead of broad adaptation gains.

related_work.title: The Illusion of Progress? A Critical Look at Test-Time Adaptation for Vision-Language Models
related_work.authors: Lijun Sheng, Jian Liang, Ran He, Zilei Wang, Tieniu Tan
related_work.how_it_relates: Motivates fairer comparisons and trustworthiness-oriented analysis, which is why FOCUS is evaluated with matched-rate gates and harmful-update metrics.

related_work.title: Advancing Reliable Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models under Visual Variations
related_work.authors: Yiwen Liang, Hui Chen, Yizhe Xiong, Zihan Zhou, Mengyao Lyu, Zijia Lin, Shuaicheng Niu, Sicheng Zhao, Jungong Han, Guiguang Ding
related_work.how_it_relates: ReTA already shows that entropy is not a sufficient reliability signal. FOCUS differs only by testing whether object-centric evidence is a better StatA update gate than a ReTA-style reliability score under the same acceptance-rate budget.

related_work.title: Adaptive Cache Enhancement for Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models
related_work.authors: Khanh-Binh Nguyen, Phuoc-Nguyen Bui, Hyunseung Choo, Duc Thanh Nguyen
related_work.how_it_relates: Establishes that conservative pseudo-label selection is already prior art, limiting FOCUS to a specific object-centric safety cue rather than a general reliability contribution.

related_work.title: Mitigating Cache Noise in Test-Time Adaptation for Large Vision-Language Models
related_work.authors: Haotian Zhai, Xinyu Chen, Can Zhang, Tianming Sha, Ruirui Li
related_work.how_it_relates: Provides another strong noise-aware reliability baseline family, reinforcing that the novelty here is not pseudo-label filtering itself.

related_work.title: Bayesian Test-Time Adaptation for Vision-Language Models
related_work.authors: Lihua Zhou, Mao Ye, Shuaifeng Li, Nianxin Li, Xiatian Zhu, Lei Deng, Hongbin Liu, Zhen Lei
related_work.how_it_relates: Represents the uncertainty-estimation route to safer VLM adaptation, contrasting with FOCUS's spatial object/background evidence.

related_work.title: Fair Context Learning for Evidence-Balanced Test-Time Adaptation in Vision-Language Models
related_work.authors: Sanggeon Yun, Ryozo Masukawa, SungHeon Jeong, Wenjun Huang, Hanning Chen, Mohsen Imani
related_work.how_it_relates: A very recent adjacent method addressing evidence imbalance and spurious context in VLM TTA, which further narrows FOCUS to the specific question of update safety in realistic online StatA streams.

related_work.title: SegDebias: Test-Time Bias Mitigation for ViT-Based CLIP via Segmentation
related_work.how_it_relates: Closest object-centric prior art because it already uses foreground/background separation for CLIP debiasing. FOCUS does not claim novelty in decomposition, only in using object-centric evidence as an online update gate.

related_work.title: GS-Bias: Global-Spatial Bias Learner for Single-Image Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models
related_work.authors: Zhaohong Huang, Yuxin Zhang, Jingjing Xie, Fei Chao, Rongrong Ji
related_work.how_it_relates: Shows that spatial cues are already being used in VLM TTA, further narrowing the novelty claim to realistic online update-safety gating.

hypothesis: In realistic online StatA adaptation, object-centric evidence from full-image, foreground, and background views predicts harmful updates better than entropy, max-softmax, and a ReTA-style consistency gate when all gates are matched to the same update acceptance rate. Using that signal to gate StatA updates will reduce drift on context-sensitive streams such as Waterbirds and CounterAnimal.

confirm: FOCUS achieves better harmful-update AUROC or AUPRC than entropy, max-softmax, and the ReTA-style CER gate in the sparse short-horizon counterfactual analysis.
FOCUS-StatA outperforms plain StatA and entropy-gated StatA on both mandatory benchmarks, Waterbirds and CounterAnimal, using two seeds.
No single simple variant such as agreement-only, foreground-only, or background-only reproduces the full gain.
The complete study fits the single RTX A6000 and eight-hour budget without relying on heavy segmentation in the main pipeline.
refute: Cheap attention-rollout masks are too noisy for the object-centric score to beat non-object-centric matched-rate gates.
Any gain disappears once acceptance rate is matched against entropy, max-softmax, or the ReTA-style CER gate.
A simple object-centric component performs as well as the full FOCUS score, making the proposed combination unnecessary.
Extra view construction overhead makes the method impractical in realistic online use.
